props.covers: The data model at the core of the interactive canvas engine: the document schema, the typed action vocabulary, the single reducer that mutates it, and the pure geometry helpers it relies on.

text.insert: The model layer is the data core of

: a FigJam-parity, typed-action, world-space interactive canvas engine, extracted into its own git submodule. It defines what a canvas document is (schema), the closed vocabulary of ways it can change (actions), the single function that performs every change (the reducer), and a small set of pure geometry helpers those changes lean on. Everything above this layer — pointer interaction, connector routing, rendering, the chrome UI, the editor shell — reads and writes through these types and functions rather than inventing its own notion of canvas state.
, is the serializable, versioned shape of a canvas:
exists today), an optional viewport (pan/zoom) and canvas size, and the validated document collections:

field (the kind decides where and how it renders), an optional
swatch pick from the closed 10-pick roster, an optional

), carry line style/arrow, their own separate
swatch pick, and may carry explicit
as a world-space polyline override that wins over auto-routing when present.
attach a note or an agent-request to an object, connection, or arbitrary region, with an
The two record types, verbatim from the schema (doc comments trimmed):
Routing is always elbow/orthogonal through the routing layer; the style field no longer selects
path algorithms. The dashed renderer applies
Load-time validation is migration-aware for old connector styles:
, and defaults any other style to

Connector creation is intentionally always gray.
. Unlike object color edits, connector color edits do not update
, so a picked connector color is not remembered for future connectors.

are the user-bend mechanism for connectors: when present, routing honors the stored world-space polyline instead of recomputing around obstacles. Reducer-wide waypoint reconciliation translates bends during rigid moves and drops them after asymmetric moves/resizes;
also clears waypoints when an endpoint object, anchor, or precise endpoint
changes, so reconnecting an endpoint returns the connector to auto-routing.

fields died in the P1/P2 cutovers); since W6 the only grouping object — members record membership via a persisted, auto-managed parentId and move with the section when it is dragged.
— added in W2, FigJam sections + V2 Flow shape vocabulary.
— added in W2; a chevron with a left/right
— added in W2, FigJam sections + V2 Flow shape vocabulary.
ellipse — added in W5; a true ellipse inscribed in the bounding box.
triangle — added in W5; an isosceles triangle with an up/down direction (defaults to up).
parallelogram — added in W5; a skewed quadrilateral with a left/right direction (defaults to right).
pentagon — added in W5; a 5-point regular polygon.
octagon — added in W5; an 8-point regular polygon.
star — added in W5; a 5-point star polygon.
plus — added in W5; a 12-point cross polygon; renders no visible label text.
chevron — added in W5; a fat-chevron polygon distinct from arrow-shape, with a left/right direction (defaults to right).
folder — added in W5; a manila-folder silhouette with a tab notch.
document-stack — added in W5; offset duplicate document silhouettes suggesting multiple stacked files.
off-page-connector — added in W5; a downward-pointing pentagon.
trapezoid — added in W5; a 4-point trapezoid (manual-operation flowchart symbol).
manual-input — added in W5; a rectangle with a slanted top edge.
hexagon — added in W5; a 6-point regular hexagon (flowchart preparation symbol).
internal-storage — added in W5; a rectangle with interior top-row and left-column rule lines.
or-junction — added in W5; a circle with an inscribed "+"; renders no visible label text.
summing-junction — added in W5; a circle with an inscribed "x"; renders no visible label text.
cylinder-horizontal — added in W5; a horizontal-orientation cylinder, distinct from database's vertical cylinder.
page-corner — added in W5; the dog-eared/folded-corner page silhouette document used before W5.
icon — added in W5; a single icon-glyph family parameterized by a required icon field (26-value CanvasIconGlyph enum), covering FigJam's Advanced picker tier.

That's 31 object types in total (the early text and source-node types were retired along the way). The D16 wave (document, database) expanded the vocabulary for reference-style architecture diagrams; the W2 wave (section, pill, arrow-shape, predefined-process) added FigJam-parity sections plus the V2 Flow shape set; D21 folded the retired Person/Chat/Chip catalog entries into the

. The W5 wave (ellipse, triangle, parallelogram, pentagon, octagon, star, plus, chevron, folder, document-stack, off-page-connector, trapezoid, manual-input, hexagon, internal-storage, or-junction, summing-junction, cylinder-horizontal, page-corner, icon) closed out FigJam's Basic/Flowchart/Advanced picker tiers — 19 native shape primitives plus one icon-glyph family (
field drawn from a 26-value CanvasIconGlyph enum) covering the Advanced tier's 26 component icons in one type instead of 26 bespoke ones.
Color: one pick from the closed 10-pick roster

with a non-empty body appended on a new line). The legacy fields were deleted from the schema and validator with no legacy read path — an old document's leftover keys are simply ignored as unknown.

is split on newlines and rendered as one line per

bullet or #/##/### heading prefix. The markdown prefix (indent plus marker) is always hidden, and

emits data-line-depth (clamped at 5) for the indent grid. The tuned geometry (hand-calibrated with design, 2026-07-08): headings anchor the left margin at 0; plain paragraphs nest 0.125em (3px) under them so body text reads as a child of its heading; bullet blocks start at 0.25em (6px) with a 0.75em glyph gutter, and each nesting level steps by that same 0.75em so a depth-d glyph starts exactly at the depth-(d-1) text column (dot/text at 24px type: 6/24, 24/42, 42/60px). Paragraph structural indent still steps a full 1em per depth on top of its 0.125em nest. Bullets choose

) — every other shape is flat/shadowless per the FigJam parity spec.

props.title: Section lock is a two-mode field with legacy-boolean migration

text.insert: rather than a boolean. "background" locks only the section frame — the section itself refuses drag and resize while its children stay movable; "all" also locks every parentId-descendant, so nothing inside the section can be dragged or resized either. Both modes are enforced at gesture-entry time by
passes "all"/"background" through, maps the legacy boolean
to "background", and turns anything else into
— so documents saved before the two-mode field keep loading instead of failing validation.
— since sections exist to wrap other objects, not to be sized like a shape.
seeds a fresh section through the unified object defaults: required
keeps the same unified text/color model when swapping object types; there are no current
schema fields to seed or clear.

pick through the section cells in
The chip is zoom-adaptive (the FigJam “big section names when zoomed out” behavior):
result that, below zoom 1, counter-scales by
— sub-linear (currently 0.6), so titles read from afar yet still shrink with the board instead of holding a constant screen size — clamped to
(6) and uniform across every section regardless of its width. A scaled chip is width-budgeted to its section's inner width (
) and ellipsizes via an inner span rather than hard-clipping at the section's
edge; at zoom ≥ 1 no transform is emitted, so the resting DOM is unchanged. The section-title editor input (
) mirrors the same scale and width budget, so in-place renames match the chip at any zoom. All knobs live on

text.insert: Membership is recorded on the member: its

), and sections nest in sections the same way — the only legal parent type, with cycles rejected by validation.

point) is a plain number pair in a single flat world-space coordinate system — logical px, no separate "world" vs. "screen" unit split baked into the schema. The origin
is not pinned to any object; a document's optional
) describes a nominal canvas footprint starting at the origin, but objects are free to have negative
always recomputes the real bounding box from the canvas-base rect plus every object's actual geometry, padded 80px by default, rather than trusting
All geometry is snapped to a
(round-to-nearest-multiple, with width/height floored at one grid cell) — this is a model-layer concern independent of the render layer's own adaptive dot-grid (
= 8, doubling/halving to stay in a comfortable on-screen pitch band as zoom changes; see
for that). The two grids are unrelated in size (16px snap vs. 8px-based adaptive dots) and serve different purposes: one is placement snapping, the other is visual density.
The default sizes in the per-type reference above (the
rows) are creation-time seeds only, chosen for a reasonable initial placement — they are not the same numbers as the pixel-sampled reference constants co-located with each def (e.g. the sticky def's
is 416x420, sampled from real FigJam exports, versus

's smaller 176x128 sticky seed). Treat the sampled, provenance-commented constants as the visual-fidelity source of truth (corner radii, stroke widths, font sizes, palette hexes) and the defaults table as just the placement convenience every
call falls back to when no explicit geometry is supplied.
moves a selection's shared bounding-box edge or center axis (left/center-x/right/top/center-y/bottom) to align every selected object along it;
(3+ objects required) spaces objects evenly between the first and last member's position along an axis, by sorting on that axis and interpolating equal gaps;
-type object to the padded (32px default) bounding box of its
-linked children. All three are pure document-in/document-out functions — no mutation, no history bookkeeping of their own — which is why the
actions exist as thin reducer cases that call them and then run the result through
, is a single discriminated union with exactly 26 variants, every one prefixed
. This closed vocabulary is the entire surface area through which a canvas document can change — nothing mutates a document by any other path. Alongside it,
plus one tool per placeable object type (
— the retired text/source-node tools died with their types).
— replaces the current selection (none / objects / connection / annotation / region).
— Sets the document's top-level title. Recorded in history like any other document mutation.
— creates one new object of a given type — geometry/
default from the type's row in
from the per-kind last-picked memory (D17) when not supplied — and selects it.
— bulk-inserts objects (and optional connections) in one history entry, remapping ids on collision; used for paste.
— patches one object's fields (geometry is re-snapped, style is shallow-merged).
— bulk geometry patch for many objects at once (drag/section-carry), with an opt-out of history recording for live-preview frames.
from geometry (the ≥60% containment rule) via the shared reconciler; dispatched by move/resize gestures on pointer-up with
— sets one object's width/height, snapped unless opted out.
— nudges every selected object by (dx, dy), snapped unless opted out.
— clones the selected objects (and connections between them) offset by +24/+24, and selects the clones.
— removes the current selection (objects, or the one selected connection) and any connections/links/annotations that referenced it.
— creates a solid/forward/gray connection between two existing objects, optionally with anchors, a label/style/arrow, and a precise relative
; it selects the new connection and records
— patches a connection after validating endpoints/anchors and rejecting self-loops. Endpoint object/anchor/position changes clear existing
only when the patch key is exactly

— drag-to-connect gesture: connects to an existing object under the drop point, or duplicates the source object at the drop point with empty text and connects to it, in one history entry; fromAnchor is optional, and Connector Mode body-drags omit it so routing auto-picks the facing side at route time.
— attaches a note or agent-request annotation to an object, connection, or region.
— shape-swaps an object to a different type in place, preserving geometry/
from the type's defaults row (a color pick is a direction, D12 — the new kind's role table decides how the carried-over pick renders).
— reparents objects into a section (or clears parent), rejecting cycles and non-section targets.
— aligns selected objects along an edge or center axis, via
— spaces three or more selected objects evenly along an axis.
— resizes a section to the padded bounding box of its parentId children. Its adopt-side sibling
assigns parentId to parentless objects overlapping the section by ≥60%.
— pops the most recent document off history.past, pushing the current document onto history.future.
— the inverse of undo, replaying a previously undone document.
— replaces the entire state with a fresh document, clearing history and selection.
Most actions record a history entry via an internal
(source, human-readable summary, and the specific object/connection/annotation ids touched) — this is what backs both the undo ledger and any "what just changed" highlight in a consuming UI.
derives history from the patch shape after validation: endpoint changes record
, a patch whose only key is
. Endpoint changes also clear any explicit
unless the patch supplies replacement waypoints.
, so the new object is a duplicate of the source type, size, color, parent, style/layout, direction/author/icon where present, centered on the drop point with
. The reducer then creates a solid/forward/gray connector to it; when
is absent, the connection stores no source anchor and routing auto-picks the facing side at route time. It selects the new object and records
is the one function that turns
into a new state, and it is the only place a document is ever mutated. Interactive UI gestures — drags, resizes, connector drops, keyboard nudges, all wired up in
values that an external agent or automation script would dispatch to make the identical change programmatically. Neither path has a shortcut around the other: a pointer drag and an agent's

call are, to the reducer, indistinguishable inputs.

This single choke point is what guarantees two properties hold regardless of an action's origin: consistent undo/history (every mutating action goes through the same
bookkeeping, so a human edit and an agent edit undo identically), and connector-waypoint reconciliation (every action that can move or resize an object flows through the same post-action

pass — see below — so no caller can accidentally leave a connection's explicit waypoints stale). Splitting the reducer, or giving any caller a way to patch the document object directly, would let these guarantees diverge quietly between human and agent-driven edits.

props.title: Waypoint reconciliation rides inside the reducer

text.insert: ) with a stale-waypoint choke point: after any action that changed the document (excluding undo/redo/reset, which restore stored documents verbatim),

compares each explicitly-waypointed connection's two endpoint owners before and after. If both owners translated by the same delta with no resize (a rigid move — a multi-select drag, a nudge, a section carrying its members), every waypoint shifts by that same delta, preserving trunk-and-branch fan shapes. If the owners moved asymmetrically or either was resized, the waypoints are dropped so the connector falls back to obstacle-avoiding auto-routing — FigJam's own re-route-on-asymmetric-move behavior. Because this runs inside the single reducer choke point, the waypoint change shares the triggering action's own history entry: one undo restores both geometry and waypoints together.
is a set of pure, DOM-free geometry functions operating purely on plain numbers and
at a 16px grid), anchor-point resolution for connection endpoints (
), bounding-box math over one or many geometries (
), align/distribute layout math, section-fit sizing, transitive section-membership walking (
is the geometric capture seed behind

: it computes which parentless objects a section should adopt purely from bounds overlap (an object qualifies once its overlap with the section's bounds reaches a threshold fraction of the object's own area). Capture is recursive — a qualifying nested section pulls in its own qualifying members transitively. It is NOT how membership is read day-to-day: membership is the persisted
walks it transitively (drag carry, delete cascade, and view-crop all read that).
Historical note: earlier versions of this package vendored a type-only
) to type code-block references into Spectre. The code-block object kind was deleted on 2026-07-09 and took the vendored type with it; the
covers the visual concept. If a Spectre code-ref object returns, it will be built fresh against the current schema rather than reviving the old type.
— whether triggered by pointer interaction, keyboard shortcuts, the chrome UI, or an external agent — must be expressed as a
. Do not hand-roll ad hoc state mutations elsewhere (e.g. mutating

directly, or writing a parallel update path that bypasses the reducer) — doing so silently breaks undo/redo history and skips the waypoint-reconciliation pass, producing state that looks fine until the next drag or undo exposes the drift.
, following the project-wide convention of keeping tests next to the source they exercise rather than in a separate top-level test tree. Three files cover this layer:

(the largest file, exercising document validation and the full action/reducer vocabulary — object and connection CRUD, selection, undo/redo, alignment/distribution, section fitting, link status resolution),
— normalization during validation and the rigid-translate vs. drop-on-asymmetric-move reconciliation behavior in the reducer), and
(the W2 wave's section capture/tint/title validation and the new V2 Flow shape vocabulary — pill, arrow-shape, predefined-process, and the CPU icon glyph).
covers how pointer gestures — drag, resize, connector-drop, section-carry — get translated into the
values this document describes, and how
dispatches them through the same reducer an external agent would use.
